title: External Flow (IV) — Correlation Pathfinder
subtitle: Pearson Correlation Between Currency Pairs via MQL5-Python Bridge
author: Christian Benjamin (lynnchris)
verdict: PARTIAL EXTRACT — correlation coefficient thresholds, rolling correlation window pattern, and correlation as a risk filter. We already have Python-native architecture; the bridge pattern is irrelevant. Correlation is useful for position sizing (don't take correlated pairs simultaneously).

core_concept.name: Pearson Correlation Between Pairs
core_concept.description: Compute Pearson correlation coefficient between closing prices of two currency pairs over N bars. Also compute rolling 50-bar window correlation to track how the relationship changes over time.
core_concept.relevance_to_creviaderiv: Correlation as risk management gate: if we already hold a position in EURUSD and correlation with GBPUSD >= 0.8, don't open a new GBPUSD position simultaneously (same directional exposure). Also useful for multi-symbol scanning: skip correlated pairs to avoid overexposure.

very_strong_positive.action: Hedge: trade one long, one short. Or avoid trading both in same direction.

moderate_positive.action: Same directional tendency. Reduce size on second position.

weak_negative.action: Mild divergence — partial hedge if trading both.

strong_negative.action: Natural hedge — moving opposite. Trading both = reduced net exposure.

architecture.mql5: CopyRates() for two pairs → JSON → WebRequest() to Flask server
architecture.python: Pandas: overall Pearson r + rolling(50).corr() → Matplotlib PNG → JSON response
architecture.our_version: All Python-native. Pandas corr() directly on broker data. No bridge needed.

test_results.insight: Overall correlation was low but recent rolling was high — shows temporary correlation bursts. Always use rolling window, not overall, for trading decisions.

components_to_build.name: CorrelationFilter
components_to_build.note: Use pandas rolling().corr() — single line. Use as position sizing gate: if rolling_r > 0.8 and already holding pair1, halve position size for pair2.

keep: Pearson correlation formula and thresholds (>0.8 = very strong, 0.5-0.8 = moderate)
Rolling window correlation (50 bars) to detect current correlation regime vs long-term
Correlation as risk/position-sizing gate: avoid simultaneous same-direction trades on r > 0.8 pairs
Observation: short-term rolling can differ sharply from overall — use rolling for live decisions
discard: MQL5-Python HTTP bridge — we have Python-native access
Matplotlib visualization (for our use case — can add if needed for reports)
improve: Expand to multi-symbol correlation matrix: all symbols vs all other symbols in our scan list
Add correlation regime detection: 'currently correlated' vs 'currently independent' as states
Position sizing rule: if portfolio_correlation_with_new_trade > 0.7: reduce position size by (1 - correlation) factor

creviaderiv_insights.position_sizing_gate: Before opening a signal, check rolling correlation with any existing open positions. If r > 0.8: halve position size (double exposure risk). If r < -0.5: full size (acts as partial hedge).
creviaderiv_insights.multi_symbol_scan: Our TradingOrchestrator scans multiple symbols. Running correlation between all active scan symbols avoids accidentally taking the same directional bet 5 times via correlated pairs.
creviaderiv_insights.rolling_vs_overall: The EUR/USD vs GBP/USD test shows overall r=0.35 but recent rolling r=0.83. This is why overall correlation is useless — use only rolling window (50 bars on M15 = ~12 hours of current correlation).
